content.text: What Data Readiness Means for Claude Use Cases

Claude's output quality is bounded by its input quality. A Claude deployment that summarises contracts will produce better summaries if the contract text is cleanly extracted and complete. A Claude deployment that answers HR policy questions will give correct answers only if the policy knowledge base covers the question asked and the content is current.

Data readiness assessment is the structured evaluation of whether the data required for a Claude use case is accessible, in the right format, of sufficient quality, appropriately sensitive-controlled, and sufficiently comprehensive to support the use case. It is conducted before engineering build begins, as part of the feasibility assessment phase.

The most common cause of Claude integration failures in production is not prompt quality or model capability — it is data quality. A model that is handed incomplete, inconsistent, or incorrectly formatted data will produce incomplete, inconsistent, or incorrect outputs, regardless of how well the prompt is written.
The Five Dimensions of Data Readiness

attrs.alt: Data readiness assessment framework: five dimensions rated on a 1–3 scale

content.text: Data readiness is assessed across five dimensions. Each is rated 1 (blocker), 2 (significant effort required), or 3 (ready). The overall readiness score is the minimum of the five dimension scores — a single blocker-rated dimension blocks the use case regardless of how well the other four dimensions score.
Dimension 1: Accessibility

Can the application that will call Claude reach the data it needs? Accessibility is a binary gate — either the data is reachable with reasonable integration effort, or it is not. Assessment questions:
API availability:
Does the data source expose an API that can be called from the Claude integration environment? If not, what is the alternative access path (direct database query, ETL extract, file export)?
Authentication:

Can the Claude integration use a service account or API key to access the data, or does it require per-user authentication? Per-user authentication requires the integration to act on behalf of the user, which adds complexity (OAuth flow, token management).
Network path:
Is there a network route from the Claude integration environment to the data source? Common blocker: data on-premise, integration in cloud, no VPN or private connectivity established.
Data sharing agreement:

Is the data owned by a different business unit or entity? If so, is there a data sharing agreement (DSA) that covers use by the Claude application? Missing DSAs are a 4–12 week delay, not a quick fix.
Dimension 2: Format Compatibility
Is the data in a form that can be effectively processed by Claude? Claude is a text model — it processes text and produces text. Anything that is not text must be converted before it can be used:
Data Format
Conversion Required
Quality Risk
Effort
Digital-native PDF (text selectable)
PDF text extraction (pdfminer, pdfplumber)
Low — structure usually preserved
Low (1–2 days)
Scanned PDF (image-only)
OCR (AWS Textract, Google DocAI, Tesseract)
Medium–High — layout, tables, and special characters may fail
Medium (1–2 weeks for pipeline + quality testing)
Word/DOCX
python-docx or pandoc conversion
Low — structure preserved
Low (1–2 days)
Excel/XLSX with data tables
Convert to markdown table or CSV summary
Medium — large tables must be summarised; Claude cannot process 50,000 rows
Low-medium (depends on table complexity)
HTML web pages
BeautifulSoup / markdownify stripping
Low for content-rich pages; high for heavily JavaScript-rendered pages
Low (1–2 days) for static; medium (1–2 weeks) for JS-rendered
Structured database (SQL)
Query → text formatting (prose summary or markdown table)
Low — controlled query output
Low (1 day per table/view)
Email (Outlook/Exchange)
MAPI or Graph API extraction, HTML strip
Low–Medium — attachments require separate handling
Low-medium (1–3 days)
Audio/Video transcripts
Speech-to-text (Whisper, AWS Transcribe) then text processing
Medium — transcription accuracy affects downstream quality
Medium (2–3 weeks for pipeline)
Dimension 3: Quality
Even accessible, correctly-formatted data may be of insufficient quality for Claude to produce reliable outputs. Quality has four sub-dimensions:
Completeness:

What fraction of records have all required fields populated? A contract knowledge base where 30% of contracts are missing the termination clause section will produce unreliable termination clause analysis. Target: > 95% completeness for critical fields.
Accuracy:

How often is the data wrong or outdated? If the HR policy knowledge base was last updated 2 years ago and policies have changed, Claude will give incorrect answers. Target: < 5% error rate on a sampled review.
Consistency:

Are terms and values used consistently? If 'customer' and 'client' and 'account' are used interchangeably across documents, retrieval for any one term will miss the others. Inconsistent terminology is a common RAG failure mode.
Currency:

How recently was the data updated? For frequently-changing domains (pricing, policies, regulations), stale data produces incorrect outputs. Establish the maximum acceptable data age for each knowledge base.

Quality assessment requires sampling — you cannot evaluate quality by looking at the data schema. Pull 50–100 representative records and manually review each quality dimension. If the sample reveals > 20% records with issues on any quality dimension, the data needs remediation before the knowledge base can be built.
Dimension 4: Sensitivity and Compliance

What are the data handling requirements for the content being sent to the Claude API? This is not a technical question but a legal and compliance question, and it must be answered by the appropriate compliance or legal team, not by the engineering team.
Classification by sensitivity level:
Sensitivity Level
Examples
API Transmission Requirements
Additional Controls
Public / Internal
Product documentation, FAQs, public policy docs
Standard HTTPS; no special requirements
None beyond standard logging
Confidential
Internal memos, unpublished reports, internal pricing
Encrypted transport; logging; API key rotation
Ensure Anthropic DPA covers internal confidential data
Personal Data (GDPR/CCPA)
Customer names, emails, ID numbers, purchase history
Confirm legal basis for transfer; DPA in place; consider PII redaction
Data minimisation; right to erasure handling; breach notification plan
Special Category Personal Data
Health, biometric, political, religious, financial data
Requires explicit legal basis; DPO review; may require Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA)
DPIA mandatory in EU; consider on-premise processing alternative
Legally Privileged
Legal advice, litigation strategy, attorney-client communications
Consult General Counsel; sending to third-party API may waive privilege
May require explicit waiver or contractual privilege protection from Anthropic

When in doubt, start with the least sensitive data subset for the pilot and expand only after compliance review is complete for higher-sensitivity tiers. Many use cases can be scoped to a non-PII subset for initial deployment.
Dimension 5: Coverage

Does the knowledge base cover the full range of questions or tasks the Claude deployment will face? Coverage gaps produce the most frustrating user experience in RAG deployments — Claude gives accurate answers when the knowledge base covers the question, but says 'I don't know' (correctly) for questions that are not covered, causing users to question the deployment's reliability.

Coverage assessment: take 50 representative examples of the actual tasks or queries the deployment will process, and manually check whether the knowledge base contains the information needed to answer each one. A coverage rate below 80% indicates a significant knowledge base gap that will degrade user experience.

# Coverage assessment for a RAG knowledge base
def assess_coverage(test_queries: list[str], knowledge_base, retrieval_fn, threshold: float = 0.70):
    results = []
    for query in test_queries:
        chunks = retrieval_fn(query, knowledge_base, top_k=5)
        # Manual or model-graded check: do these chunks contain the answer?
        has_answer = any_chunk_contains_answer(query, chunks)  # your evaluation function
        results.append({
            "query": query,
            "chunks_retrieved": len(chunks),
            "has_answer": has_answer,
        })

    covered = sum(r["has_answer"] for r in results)
    coverage_rate = covered / len(test_queries)
    gaps = [r["query"] for r in results if not r["has_answer"]]

    return {
        "coverage_rate": coverage_rate,
        "covered": covered,
        "total": len(test_queries),
        "gap_queries": gaps,
        "passes_threshold": coverage_rate >= threshold,
    }

# A coverage rate below 0.70 means > 30% of realistic queries will be unanswerable.
# Identify the gap categories and add content to the knowledge base before launch.

attrs.alt: Knowledge base construction pipeline: five stages from source data to evaluated knowledge base

content.text: The Grounding Feasibility Assessment

'Grounding' refers to the practice of constraining Claude's responses to a specific knowledge base rather than relying on its training data. Grounding is the mechanism that makes Claude's outputs organisation-specific rather than generic. Grounding feasibility assessment asks: can this knowledge base be built, and will it produce reliable retrieval?
Retrieval Quality Metrics
A RAG knowledge base must be evaluated for retrieval quality before a Claude integration can be considered deployment-ready. The key metrics:
Precision@K:

Of the top K chunks retrieved for a query, what fraction are relevant? Target: Precision@5 ≥ 0.70 (at least 3.5 of the top 5 chunks are relevant). Below 0.50 means the retrieval is polluting the context with irrelevant content.
Recall@K:
Of all relevant chunks in the knowledge base for a query, what fraction are in the top K? Target: Recall@10 ≥ 0.60 for factual Q&A use cases. Below 0.40 means relevant content is being missed.
Answer faithfulness:

When Claude generates an answer using retrieved chunks, what fraction of claims in the answer are supported by the retrieved context? Target: > 0.85 for factual use cases. Below 0.70 indicates Claude is hallucinating beyond the retrieved context.
Answer relevance:
Is Claude's answer actually answering the question asked? Target: > 0.90. Answer relevance failures indicate a prompt instruction problem, not a retrieval problem.

# Retrieval quality evaluation (using a labelled test set)
# test_set: list of {"query": str, "relevant_doc_ids": list[str]}
# retrieve_fn: function(query) -> list[{"id": str, "text": str, "score": float}]
def evaluate_retrieval(test_set, retrieve_fn, k=5):
    precision_scores = []
    recall_scores = []

    for item in test_set:
        retrieved = retrieve_fn(item["query"])[:k]
        retrieved_ids = {r["id"] for r in retrieved}
        relevant_ids = set(item["relevant_doc_ids"])

        precision = len(retrieved_ids & relevant_ids) / k
        recall = len(retrieved_ids & relevant_ids) / len(relevant_ids) if relevant_ids else 0

        precision_scores.append(precision)
        recall_scores.append(recall)

    avg_precision = sum(precision_scores) / len(precision_scores)
    avg_recall = sum(recall_scores) / len(recall_scores)

    return {
        f"precision@{k}": round(avg_precision, 3),
        f"recall@{k}": round(avg_recall, 3),
        "f1": round(2 * avg_precision * avg_recall / (avg_precision + avg_recall + 1e-9), 3),
        "passes_threshold": avg_precision >= 0.70,
        "n_queries": len(test_set),
    }
Chunking Strategy Impact on Retrieval Quality
The chunking strategy — how documents are split into pieces for the vector index — has a large impact on retrieval quality. Common chunking strategies and their trade-offs:
Strategy
Description
Best For
Risk
Fixed-size (character or token)
Split every N tokens with M% overlap
Homogeneous prose documents with even density
Splits sentences and clauses mid-thought; reduces coherence
Semantic (paragraph/section)
Split at natural boundaries (paragraphs, headings)
Structured documents with clear sections (policies, contracts)
Section length variance can produce very short or very long chunks
Sliding window with overlap
Fixed size but each chunk overlaps previous by 20%
Dense information documents where context bleeds across chunk boundaries
Increased storage and embedding cost from duplicate content
Hierarchical (parent-child)
Embed child chunks for retrieval, expand to parent for context
Documents where relevant information is scattered across related sections
More complex retrieval logic; requires parent-child metadata

For most enterprise use cases, semantic chunking at the paragraph or section level with a target of 300–600 tokens per chunk produces the best balance of retrieval precision and context coherence. Test at least two chunking strategies on your actual documents before committing to the production pipeline.
Data Readiness Remediation Paths

When a data readiness assessment identifies gaps, the programme team must decide between three options: remediate the data before proceeding, redesign the use case to avoid the gap, or descope the use case pending remediation.
Readiness Gap
Remediation Path
Timeline
When to Descope Instead
DSA missing
Initiate DSA through legal; interim: use anonymised subset for pilot
4–12 weeks
If DSA requires executive sign-off across business units and timeline is unacceptable
PDF extraction failures (scanned)
Upgrade OCR pipeline; use AWS Textract or Google DocumentAI; manually fix critical documents
2–6 weeks
If > 50% of documents are scanned and OCR quality is too low for the use case accuracy requirement
Stale knowledge base
Establish content refresh cadence; identify owner; build automated update pipeline
2–4 weeks for first refresh; then ongoing
If no single owner exists for the knowledge base and content governance cannot be established
Coverage gaps (< 70%)
Identify gap topics; source or author content for each; add to knowledge base
Depends on gap size; 2–8 weeks
If gap topics require expert knowledge that is not documented and cannot be authored at scale
Completeness failures
Data cleaning job to populate missing fields; engage source system owner; manual remediation for critical records
2–6 weeks
If completeness failures are systemic (the source system does not capture required data) and no fix is planned
Running a Data Readiness Assessment: Step-by-Step

A data readiness assessment is a structured 1–2 day activity involving the programme engineer, the BU data owner, and optionally the data engineering team. It produces a Data Readiness Report that feeds into the use case feasibility decision.
Identify all required data sources:

List every data source the Claude integration needs to function. For a contract review use case: the contract repository (SharePoint or document management system), the approved contract template library, and any policy documents that govern acceptable contract terms.
Interview the data owner for each source:

A 30-minute conversation per source covering: how is the data stored, what format, how current, who owns it, are there access restrictions, and are there data quality issues the owner is already aware of?
Pull a sample:
Extract 50–100 representative records from each data source. For document repositories, this means 50–100 actual documents — not folder names or metadata only.
Score each dimension for each source:
Rate accessibility, format, quality, sensitivity, and coverage on the 1–3 scale using the sampled records as evidence. Document the evidence for each rating.
Identify blockers and estimate resolution effort:
For any dimension rated 1, describe the specific blocker and estimate the time and resources required to resolve it.
Produce the Data Readiness Report:

A 2–4 page structured document covering the overall rating, dimension-by-dimension findings, identified blockers with resolution paths, and a recommendation: proceed, proceed conditionally, or descope.
The Knowledge Base Maintenance Problem
A knowledge base that passes the readiness assessment at launch will degrade over time if content is not maintained. Enterprise knowledge bases have three common decay patterns:
Policy and Process Drift

Organisational policies, pricing, and processes change. If the knowledge base is not updated when source documents change, Claude will give answers based on outdated information. Users who discover this — even once — will stop trusting the system.

Prevention: assign a knowledge base owner for each content domain. The owner is responsible for reviewing changes to source documents and triggering knowledge base updates. Integrate with document management system change notifications where possible to alert the owner automatically.
Coverage Decay Through Scope Creep

As the Claude deployment proves useful, users ask questions outside the original scope. Questions about topics not in the knowledge base produce 'I don't have information on that' responses, which frustrate users who expect the system to know everything in the domain.

Prevention: track all 'no answer found' events in the retrieval pipeline. Weekly review of these events identifies the most common coverage gaps. Prioritise content additions based on query frequency — answer the most-asked unanswered questions first.
Retrieval Drift from Index Growth

As more content is added to the knowledge base, the retrieval index becomes larger and precision may decrease — more chunks compete for the top-K slots, and the signal-to-noise ratio decreases. A knowledge base that had Precision@5 of 0.85 at launch may drop to 0.65 after 6 months of unchecked content additions.

Prevention: re-run the retrieval evaluation (Precision@5, Recall@10) monthly. When metrics drop below threshold, investigate: Is new content poorly formatted? Are there duplicate or near-duplicate chunks inflating the index? Is the chunking strategy still appropriate for the new content types?
Data Readiness for Non-RAG Use Cases
Data readiness is not only a RAG concern. Direct API and tool use patterns also require data readiness assessment, though the dimensions are different:
Direct API Use Cases (Pattern A)
For direct API use cases, the 'data' is the content the user provides per request. Data readiness questions focus on:
Input validation:

What input validation is in place to ensure the content sent to Claude is well-formed? If users can submit empty requests, extremely long requests, or requests in unexpected languages, the integration must handle these gracefully.
Content sensitivity:

Can users paste sensitive content (PII, confidential client data, trade secrets) into the prompt? If so, what governance controls exist to prevent inappropriate submission? Who is responsible for ensuring users understand what can and cannot be submitted?
Output handling:
Where does Claude's output go? If it is stored (in a CRM, document system, or email), the data sensitivity of the output must match the sensitivity of the system it is stored in.
Tool Use and Agentic Use Cases (Patterns C and D)
For use cases where Claude reads from or writes to external systems via tools, the data readiness assessment expands to include:
Schema documentation:

Every system Claude queries via tool use must have documented schema and field semantics. Claude cannot reliably interpret an undocumented database schema — it will make incorrect assumptions about field meanings.
Data quality at the API level:

The data returned by tool calls must be clean and consistently formatted. If a CRM API sometimes returns customer names as 'Last, First' and sometimes as 'First Last', Claude will inconsistently process the output.
Write scope and rollback:

For use cases where Claude writes to external systems, the data readiness assessment must document what Claude can write, what it cannot write, and how incorrect writes can be identified and reversed.
Integrating Data Readiness into the Use Case Canvas

The Use Case Canvas — the structured template used to move a use case from ideation through feasibility — should include a Data Readiness section that captures the five dimension scores and the identified blockers. This section is completed during the technical feasibility review (typically 2 weeks after the ideation workshop) and is a prerequisite for the use case entering the pilot intake queue.
The Data Readiness section of the canvas includes:
Data sources required (list, with source system and owner for each)
Accessibility rating (1–3) and evidence
Format compatibility rating (1–3) and evidence
Quality rating (1–3) and evidence (sample size, error rate found)
Sensitivity classification and compliance status (DPA confirmed / pending / required)
Coverage rate for RAG use cases (% of test queries answered by knowledge base)
Overall readiness rating (minimum of dimension scores)
Blockers identified and resolution timeline
Recommended action: proceed / proceed conditionally pending [specific action] / descope

A use case canvas with an incomplete Data Readiness section should not enter the pilot intake queue. The readiness assessment is not optional — it is the mechanism that prevents engineering teams from discovering fundamental data blockers mid-build.
Measuring Data Readiness Programme-Wide

At the programme level, data readiness scores across all use cases in the backlog reveal systemic data infrastructure problems that individual use case assessments surface but cannot solve. Common systemic patterns:
Multiple use cases blocked on the same legacy system:

Five use cases are blocked because a specific legacy system has no API. This is a platform investment that should be escalated to IT leadership — not solved five times independently for five separate use cases.
PII in every relevant knowledge base:

Every high-value use case involves personal data, and the DPA/DPIA process is adding 6–10 weeks to every deployment. The programme should invest in a reusable PII redaction service that de-risks all future use cases simultaneously.
Knowledge base ownership gap:

Multiple use cases need knowledge bases for domains that have no designated content owner. This is a content governance problem that the programme cannot solve technically — it requires business leadership to designate owners.

Aggregating data readiness scores across the use case backlog and identifying systemic patterns is a programme health metric that should be reported in the quarterly business review alongside the ROI metrics and deployment progress.
Summary

Data readiness assessment evaluates five dimensions — accessibility, format compatibility, quality, sensitivity/compliance, and coverage — against a 1–3 rating scale where a single blocker-rated dimension prevents the use case from proceeding. For RAG use cases, grounding feasibility assessment adds retrieval quality evaluation (Precision@5 ≥ 0.70, Recall@10 ≥ 0.60) as a readiness gate before production deployment.

The knowledge base construction pipeline moves from source data through extraction, cleaning, chunking, embedding, and retrieval evaluation — with readiness gates at each stage. Chunking strategy is the most impactful design decision in the pipeline and should be evaluated empirically on representative documents before committing to the production approach. When data readiness gaps are identified, the programme team chooses between remediation, use case redesign, or descoping — with clear criteria for when descoping is the right call rather than the easy one.
